questions.question:
What does the following C-statement declare?

int (*f) (int *);

A. A function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer
B. A function that takes an integer as argument and returns an integer pointer
C. A pointer to a function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer
D. A function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns a function pointer
questions.answer: C. A pointer to a function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer
questions.to_find: What type does the C declaration `int (*f)(int *);` describe?

understand.plain: This question tests understanding of C declaration syntax. The declaration `int (*f)(int *);` uses parentheses around `*f` to bind the pointer operator to `f` first, making `f` a pointer to a function. That function accepts an integer pointer (`int *`) as its argument and returns an `int`.

keywords.explain: A variable that stores the address of a function, allowing indirect calls to it.
In C, parentheses around `*name` override the default binding so `*` attaches to the name before the function `()` does.
keywords.example: `int *f(int)` is a function returning int*; `int (*f)(int)` is a pointer to a function returning int

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='540' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='540' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='270' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>int  (*f)  (int *);</text><rect x='10' y='50' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>int</text><text x='40' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>return type</text><rect x='120' y='50' width='80' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='160' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(*f)</text><text x='160' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>pointer to func</text><rect x='250' y='50' width='100' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(int *)</text><text x='300' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>takes int* arg</text><text x='270' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>f is a pointer to a function: takes int* → returns int</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Annotated breakdown of `int (*f)(int *);` showing: `int` = return type, `(*f)` = f is a pointer to a function, `(int *)` = the function takes an integer pointer argument.

given.aim: Identify the type of `f` in the declaration `int (*f)(int *);`

terms.meaning: C declaration; parentheses around *f bind the star to f before the function-call parentheses
terms.example: compare with int *f(int*) where f would be a function returning int*
terms.connects: The binding order determines whether f is a pointer-to-function or a function returning a pointer

terms.meaning: The leftmost `int` is the type the pointed-to function returns
terms.connects: Confirms option C (returns integer, not a pointer)

terms.meaning: The argument list `(int *)` says the pointed-to function accepts one integer pointer
terms.example: f = &scanf_wrapper; where the wrapper takes int*
terms.connects: Distinguishes options A/B/C on what the argument type is

given.plan: Apply the C declaration reading rule: start from the identifier, go right (function call), then left (pointer), then use outer context for return type. `(*f)` makes f a pointer; `(int *)` is its parameter; `int` is its return type.

steps.title: Read the declaration using the right-left (spiral) rule
steps.apply: Start at `f`. Move right: blocked by `)`. Move left inside parens: `*f` — so `f` is a pointer. Move right past `)`: `(int *)` — pointer to a function taking `int*`. Move left: `int` — that function returns `int`.
steps.note: Without the outer parentheses `(*f)`, `f` would be a function returning `int*`, not a pointer to function.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply: f is a POINTER TO A FUNCTION (eliminates A, B, D which call f a 'function'). The function takes `int*` and returns `int`. This matches option C exactly.
steps.note: Option D is a distractor about returning a function pointer, which would require syntax like `int (*(*f)(int*))()`.

solution.result: C. A pointer to a function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer

questions.formula_note: C declaration reading rule (right-left/spiral rule): parentheses around `*identifier` make it a pointer-to-function; the function's parameter list and return type are read from the remaining context.

questions.to_find: Which of the five statements about the swap function are correct?

understand.plain: The swap function uses arithmetic (subtract/add) instead of a temporary variable to exchange two integers via pointers. The question asks which statements correctly describe its behavior. The arithmetic trick works only when px != py (i.e., the pointers are different); if both pointers point to the same variable, the result is zero, not a swap. Also, if null pointers are passed, a segmentation fault can occur.

keywords.explain: A technique to swap two values without a temp variable using arithmetic: a=a-b; b=a+b; a=b-a. Works when a and b are different variables.
When two pointers point to the same memory location. Arithmetic/XOR swap fails with aliased pointers because both reads and writes go to the same cell.
keywords.example: swap(&x, &x): *px=*py so *px-*py=0, then *py=0+0=0, *px=0-0=0. x becomes 0, not unchanged.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='520' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Arithmetic Swap — two cases</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='220' height='140' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>px != py (works)</text><text x='130' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a=3, b=5</text><text x='130' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*px = 3-5 = -2</text><text x='130' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*py = -2+5 = 3</text><text x='130' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*px = 3-(-2) = 5</text><text x='130' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Result: a=5, b=3 ✓</text><rect x='280' y='40' width='220' height='140' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='390' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>px == py (fails)</text><text x='390' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a=3 (same cell)</text><text x='390' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*px = 3-3 = 0</text><text x='390' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*py = 0+0 = 0</text><text x='390' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*px = 0-0 = 0</text><text x='390' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Result: a=0 ✗</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two cases: when px != py the arithmetic swap correctly exchanges values (shown with a=3,b=5 becoming a=5,b=3). When px == py (aliasing), the value becomes 0 instead of being preserved.

given.aim: Determine which statements S1-S5 correctly describe the behavior of the arithmetic swap function

terms.meaning: Using a=a-b; b=a+b; a=b-a to swap without a temporary variable
terms.example: Equivalent to XOR swap but using +/- arithmetic
terms.connects: Works correctly only when the two pointers differ (no aliasing)

terms.meaning: Dereferencing a NULL pointer causes a segmentation fault on most systems
terms.connects: Supports S2: the function may segfault if px or py is NULL

terms.meaning: If both pointers point to the same integer, the swap destroys the value (result = 0)
terms.connects: Supports S4: works for some (non-aliased) valid pointers but not all (aliased case fails)

given.plan: Check each statement: S1 — is there a compile error? No, the code is syntactically valid C. S2 — can it segfault? Yes, with NULL pointers. S3 — correct for ALL pointers? No, aliased pointers break it. S4 — correct for SOME but not ALL? Yes, fails when px==py. S5 — does it mix integer and pointer arithmetic? No, only integer arithmetic on *px and *py.

steps.title: Check S1 — compilation error
steps.apply: The code uses standard C pointer dereferencing and integer arithmetic. There is no syntax error or type mismatch. S1 is FALSE.
steps.note: The code compiles cleanly with any standard C compiler.

steps.title: Check S2 — segmentation fault possibility
steps.apply: If px or py is NULL (or any invalid pointer), dereferencing it with `*px` or `*py` causes a segmentation fault at runtime. The function does no NULL check. S2 is TRUE.
steps.note: NULL is a 'valid' pointer value (it compiles), but dereferencing it is undefined behavior and typically causes SIGSEGV.

steps.title: Check S3 and S4 — correctness for aliased inputs
steps.apply:
Case 1 (px != py): Let *px=a, *py=b. After line 1: *px=a-b. After line 2: *py=(a-b)+b=a. After line 3: *px=a-(a-b)=b. Swap works.
Case 2 (px == py, same memory cell): Let *px=*py=a. Line 1: *px=a-a=0. Line 2: *py=0+0=0. Line 3: *px=0-0=0. Value destroyed — swap fails.
So S3 (correct for ALL) is FALSE; S4 (correct for some but not all) is TRUE.
steps.note: Passing the same variable twice is a valid call since both &x and &x are accessible memory locations — the function just doesn't handle this edge case.

steps.title: Check S5 — mixing integers and pointers
steps.apply: All arithmetic operates on `*px` and `*py` which are integers (int). No pointer arithmetic is performed. S5 is FALSE.
steps.note: Pointer arithmetic would look like px + 1 (advancing the pointer); here we only dereference and do integer math.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: True statements: S2 (segfault possible) and S4 (works for some but not all). This matches option C.
steps.note: Option B (S2 and S3) is wrong because S3 requires correctness for ALL pointers, which fails for aliased inputs.

questions.formula_note: Arithmetic (no-temp) swap idiom: a=a-b; b=a+b; a=b-a. Correct only when the two operands occupy different memory locations; fails with aliased pointers (px==py causes the value to become zero).

questions.answer: D. On execution, the value printed is 5 more than the integer value entered
questions.to_find: What is the output behavior of the given C program that uses a pointer with scanf and printf?

understand.plain: The program declares an integer `i` and a pointer `pi` that holds the address of `i`. It reads an integer from stdin into `*pi` (which is the same as reading into `i`), then prints `i + 5`. Since `pi` points to `i`, scanf stores the input directly in `i`, and the output is that input value plus 5.

keywords.explain: `int *pi = &i;` makes `pi` hold the memory address of `i`. Dereferencing `*pi` accesses the same storage as `i`.
scanf expects a pointer to where it should store the scanned value. Passing `pi` (which already holds `&i`) is identical to passing `&i`.
keywords.example: scanf("%d", pi); reads an int and stores it at the address pi holds, which is &i, so i gets the value.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='480' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><rect x='60' y='50' width='100' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>i</text><text x='110' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(value)</text><rect x='280' y='50' width='140' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>pi</text><text x='350' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>= &i (address of i)</text><line x1='280' y1='80' x2='160' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='240' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>scanf("%d", pi) stores input into i; printf prints i+5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory diagram: pi is a pointer holding the address of i. scanf uses pi to write the scanned integer directly into i. printf then outputs i+5.

given.aim: Determine the runtime behavior and output of the C program using a pointer passed to scanf

terms.meaning: pi is initialized to point to i; pi holds the address of i
terms.example: if i is at address 1000, pi == 1000
terms.connects: Means scanf(pi) is the same as scanf(&i), storing the scanned value in i

terms.meaning: Reads a decimal integer from stdin and stores it at the address given by pi, which is &i
terms.connects: i now holds the entered value; no error because pi is a valid int* pointer

terms.meaning: Prints the integer value of i plus 5
terms.connects: Output is 5 more than the entered integer, confirming option D

given.plan: Trace the program: `pi = &i` (pi points to i). `scanf("%d", pi)` reads an integer into i (same as scanf("%d", &i)). `printf("%d\n", i+5)` prints i+5. No compilation error, no runtime error, and the output is the entered value + 5.

steps.title: Check for compilation issues
steps.apply: `int *pi = &i;` is valid C — pi is an int pointer initialized with the address of i. `scanf("%d", pi);` expects an int* for %d; pi is int*, so types match. `printf("%d\n", i+5);` is standard. No compilation error. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: In older C (pre-C99), `main()` without explicit return type defaults to int, so this still compiles.

steps.title: Trace execution
steps.apply:
Step 1: `pi = &i` — pi now holds address of i.
Step 2: `scanf("%d", pi)` — since pi == &i, this reads an integer from stdin and stores it in i. Say user enters N, so i = N.
Step 3: `printf("%d\n", i + 5)` — prints N + 5.
No segfault, no undefined behavior for valid integer input. Option B is FALSE.
steps.note: The address of a local variable is always a valid accessible pointer within its scope.

steps.title: Eliminate C and confirm D
steps.apply: Option C says the printed value is 5 more than the ADDRESS of i. But printf uses `i + 5` where i is the integer VALUE stored at that address, not the address itself. Option C is FALSE. Option D — printed value is 5 more than the entered integer — matches our trace exactly. D is TRUE.
steps.note: To print the address, you would need `printf("%p\n", (void*)pi)` or similar.

solution.result: D. On execution, the value printed is 5 more than the integer value entered

questions.formula_note: Pointer indirection: `int *pi = &i` makes pi an alias for i's address; passing pi to scanf is identical to passing &i, so the scanned integer is stored directly in i.
